name: Frank Johnson Goodnow, LL.D.
description: SUCCESSOR-ELECT TO DANIEL COIT GILMAN AND IRA REMSEN.

name: Griffin, Edward Herrick
motto: "—— not terrible, That I should fear, nor sociably mild, But solemn and sublime."
major: Professor of the History of Philosophy and Dean of the College Faculty

name: Ames, Joseph Sweetman
motto: "One of the Heavenly host, and, by his gait, None of the meanest—some great Potentate Or of the Thrones above, such majesty Invests him coming."
major: Professor of Physics and Director of the Physical Laboratory

name: Herrick Griffin, D., LL. D.
motto: "Possible, without fear, nor socially inevitable and sublime."
major: the History of Philosophy; Dean of the College Faculty.

name: John Calvin French, Ph. D.
motto: "Work is my recreation, the play of faculty; a delight like that which a bird feels in flying, or a fish in darting through the water."

name: Herbert Eveleth Greene, Ph. D.
motto: "Tutored by thee, hence poetry exalts her voice to ages; and informs the page with music, image, sentiment, and thought, never to die."

name: Kirby Flower Smith
major: PH. D., LL. D., PROFESSOR OF LATIN
description: HONORARY PRESIDENT OF THE CLASS OF 1914 ... seen with its numbers, reason for this self-satisfaction could not escape the day that the rough stunts at... probably as that we chose football team, followed by the "OUS" article, all game and the history of both events,— rush by seven... ibly due more to reserves being down. Spectacular several times, quarter recovered interference, played a hard, ph's goal for Freshmen. son e an, Ray Breuninger (Capt.) Breuninger rivalry, but way. It is its speakers.

name: Barton, Alexander Kirkland
motto: "Tantum religio potuit suadere malorum." —Lucretius.
clubs: Recording Secretary, Y. M. C. A., J.
Chairman Mission Study Committee, J., S.
description: What shall we say of Aleck? Some fellows say he is good, some say he is bad, all say he has an individuality of his own. He hasn't been asleep at Hopkins. He has heard a great deal, said little, and worked like a Trojan—i. e., in college activities. The Dean shook his head, folded his hands, looked calmly at the Faculty, and said in a low, puzzled voice, "Why, gentlemen, this is most unfortunate!" when it was proposed by Dr. Brush to give Aleck a watch-key. (This state secret leaked out through Dr. Greene's indignation at the suggestion.) Aleck is good-looking, and has the complexion of a Pikesville rosebush on a May morning. This comes from his good habits. Aleck is crafty; for, when he stays out all night he goes home in a taxi like a gentleman, and only his intimates realize how often he sees the dawn dapple the East. Of Aleck's future success we can have no doubt—he will conquer all obstacles or die in the attempt. No proposition is too great for him to undertake; and, though he may rush in where angels fear to tread, the devil himself cannot get ahead of him.

name: Bowden, David Thomas
motto: "Could I love less, I should be happier." — Bailey.
description: Davy has been with us now four years, and we venture a guess that no more than half of the class knows of his existence. By this we mean, gentle readers, that he is quiet, and of the whole University loves his own company best. To be sure, on certain occasions he mixes in with other fellows; for instance, when the Glee Club gives a concert, or even possibly when there is a dance at McCoy Hall; but it is rare, very rare, that he condescends to allow us to enjoy his company. But what does he do with himself? Ah, that’s the question. We wonder whether he studies hard (his marks don’t show his efforts); of course, there might be a lady in the case—but really he is too young. It has been said that Davy intends to study medicine. God help his patients.

name: Bridgman, Victor Horace
motto: "Painter, this likeness is too strong."—Cooper.
description: Lo, poor Vic Bridgman! whose untamed mind Revels in rude roughhouse of every kind; In turmoil, riot, rushes, anarchy, Chairs overturned while chalk and rubbers fly, And loud resound through Hopkins' cloistered hall Guy's wrathful threat and Merrick's injured bawl. Hates even the quiet of examination, And hails a chance for its assassination, By making paper bomb-shells and belaboring The unsuspecting heads of students neighboring. Such gentle sports as football and lacrosse For exercise still left him at a loss, So off to Panama he took his flight The Atlantic and Pacific to unite, And help them blow up tons and tons of dynamite. We shall not be surprised if it transpire That some day Vic should set the Thames on fire.

name: Catlin, Herbert Edgar
motto: "It is a wondrous thing how fleet 'Twas on those little silver feet; With what a pretty skipping grace It oft would challenge to the race; For it was swifter much than hinds And trod as if on the four winds." —Marvell. "Jam jam futurus rusticus." —Horace.
description: Herbert,—short, with light hair and light feet. He gets lit on the quarter and literally lights out like lightning. He got so lit one night in the Armory that he lit out his light feet and lit in at the finish one flicker ahead of Virginia's last man, giving Johns Hopkins a chance to get lit—not Eng. Lit.

name: Laurence B. Chenoweth
motto: "Cur me querellis exanimas tuis?"—Horace.
clubs: Member, Chess Club, F., So., J., S.
Committee on Arrangements, Inter-Class Debate, S.
description: Chen's physical make-up presents some strange contrasts. He has shoulders like Frank Gotch, a cute little stomach like the Dean's, and a complexion, which, to use an antique simile, is suggestive of "roses swimming in milk." Last, but not least, he has a gait like Joe Ames. His performances as a football player, as a laboratory assistant in Biology (he gives daily free lectures and demonstrations in this subject) and as a public speaker are all of a high order. What Chen does not know about Shakespeare and the drama in general is not worth knowing. In Lit. VIII he uncorks an amount of theatrical knowledge which amazes his fellow students and turns them and the professor green with envy. His future, without doubt, will be upon the boards; and twenty years hence we shall be among the admiring thousands who will flock to acclaim his unrivalled interpretations of Malvolio or Sir John Falstaff.

name: Coblentz, Richard Gilmore
motto: "The hidden soul of harmony."——Milton
clubs: Member, Glee Club, F., So., J. S.
Member, Instrumental Club, So., J., S.
description: The hidden soul of harmony.—Milton. "Rich" has grown quite serious and sober during his Senior year. We are not sure whether this thoughtfulness has been due to the weight of Senior dignity or to the care of the Musical Clubs on the Christmas trip. We have one chief criticism of "Rich"—he goes with Walter Geissel (v. p. 47), and when these two approach a poor victim, one on either side, life is not worth living. In Biology I and the Dean's class, "Coby" has tricks of his own. The class never tires of witnessing Dr. Andrew's defeat in each successive quiz, when Rich meets his most carefully planned question with "I haven't come to that in laboratory yet, Doctor." The good old Dean long ago succumbed to that searching pair of eyes right under his whiskers, and no longer dares look in their direction, much less address a question to their owner. Rich was never known to break the immobile placidity of his features in the Dean's class. As a musician "Our Manager" is indispensable to the Instrumental Club, for were it not for the wooing and mournful tones of his clarinet, it is doubtful whether the other players could maintain their proverbial sadness of countenance.

name: Corner, Henry Evans
motto: "He never yet no villainy ne said In all his life, unto no manner of wight. He was a very perfect gentle knight." —Chaucer.
description: The busiest man in the class. He is forever holding conferences, receiving mail, and arranging matters generally—all this in the intervals between classes, study, and his manifold literary engagements. He is one of those marvels for whom the noblest word is duty, and who consequently have duties piled upon them from all sides. The insistence of his industry is appalling. An aristocrat by nature, though he practices democracy; the cool, Platonic superiority stands always behind. This adds the required touch to his winsome hazel eyes and delicately poetic profile to make them truly irresistible. Guaranteed to be without flaw and to work to your complete satisfaction. But beware of the anger of a patient man; he has a quick flame of his own, and, we suspect, could "swear like an angel," though the most on record is a damn.

name: Francis Asbury Davis
motto: "Proicit ampullas et sesquipedalia verba." —Horace.
clubs: Member Chess Club, F., So., J., S.
description: Frank is the most imperturbably good-natured member of the class. When asked by irate instructors why he was late for nine o'clock classes he relies with admirable sang froid, "Why, Doctor, I tarried to eat an extra bun for breakfast," or "I was detained, owing to a disinclination to arise this morning." He is an ardent follower of athletics, and rarely, until his Senior year, failed to engage two seats for each contest. His impending graduation or some other cause has cast a shadow over his last year; and at times his friends have observed a far-away look, or an expression of mute suffering in those trustful eyes. Frank's courses under Doctor Greene have influenced him more than any others, for his phraseology is of a decidedly antique and picturesque cast, which reminds one of Tottel's Miscellany or Burton's Anatomy of Melancholy. Frank is looking forward to a business career, and we take great pleasure in recommending him as well qualified for the arduous duties of a floor-walker.

name: George Edwin Dorsey
motto: "Great talk of revolution. German soldiers, camps, confusion, Tumults, lotteries, rage, delusion, Gin, suicide, and Methodism." —Shelley. "At best a contradiction still." —Pope.
clubs: Usher, Musical Clubs' Concert, So., J.
Assistant Manager, Track Team, So., J.
Secretary, Johns Hopkins-Fifth Regiment Games Committee, S.
Member, Third Championship Meet, S. A. I. A. A. Committee, S.
description: Extreme, violent, blunt, self-reliant, egotistical, generous, impulsive; makes friends and enemies regardless; despite his rampant frankness one never knows whether to take him seriously or not, for he loves to watch the puppets dance, without the least concern for the puppets or their opinion of him; blunt and tactful on occasion (of his own choice), mocking, candid in like and dislike; lover of nature and Wordsworth, and hence by Mother Greene called the Philosopher; a hero worshipper and iconoclast in one; talks about Iroquois but delights in strawberry ice-cream sodas; loves to smash day-dreams, but is forever building castles in the air; creatively romantic but critically practical; a most impossible combination of Hamlet and Laertes.

name: Dorsey, John Lanahan
motto: "This fellow is wise enough to play the fool, And to do that well craves a kind of wit." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Varsity Football Team, F., So.; Football, "H.," So.; Varsity Baseball Team, F., So., J., S.; Baseball "H.," F., J.; Manager Football Team, S.; Football, H. M.; Captain Freshman Football Team; Manager Sophomore Football Team; Manager Sophomore Baseball Team; Class Numerals; Banquet Committee, F., So.; Chairman, So.; Banquet Speaker, F., So., J., S.; Head Usher, J. H. U.-Fifth Reg. Meet, J.; Usher Commencement, So., J.; Senior Week Committee.
description: Although he has a somewhat too delicate chin and his eyes are uncertain, he is one of our most charming young men. Ladies will please take notice.

name: Wallace Roylance Everton
motto: "And both were young and one was fair."——Byron.
description: Stunning girl—fine old family—pa rich—handed mitten for gay young blade — terrific blow—haggard look—distressed air—harassed mien—(see picture)—lacrosse player—still game — sends congratulations to happy pair—yet deep in woe—determines to end it all—1913, Physics I—1914, Philosophy I—1915, will go to theological school.

name: Walter Frederick Geissel
motto: "A skillful distribution of sweet sounds." —Wordsworth.
clubs: Member, Instrumental Club, F., So., J., S.
Member, Music Committee, Interclass and Intercollegiate Debates, S.
description: Walter invariably arrives at the University in an irritating perfection of neatness. The result is frequently a grand rough-house. Then comes the most exasperating part,—for while the aggressor is vainly attempting to restore a part to his disheveled locks and fondly nursing two or three injured ribs, Walter carelessly slides a hand back over his obedient hair, adjusts his glasses and their cute little black ribbon, gives his shoulders a hitch or two, and smilingly appears as undisturbed as before. He has been seen to stroll contentedly down Howard Street about matinee time, and, well—he was not alone. We can well understand his charm, for we, too, have felt the sway of his fiddle. It is as a bass soloist, however, that he is most remarkable. After he had rendered "When the Bell in the Belfry Rings Ding, Dong," the leader of the Glee Club had great difficulty in reassembling his singers—some having fled, others being still lost in amazement as to how he did it and got up out of the cellar again. Walter intends entering upon some professional career—we have no hesitancy in recommending him as an elevator-man in the University.

name: Lawrence Getz
motto: "He was a man of an unbounded stomach." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Season Ticket Committee, So., J., S.
description: Our Business Manager, the Indefatigable Getz. "Solid as the tread of time," and as direct, with him you must know what you are talking about and stand ready to prove. When Lawrence begins to dig, the roots of the subject shrivel in despair, nay the very foundations of the earth tremble. He will know the roll-call of all the bones in a fish skeleton, or the patron saints of every country and tribe under heaven, or perish in the attempt—and he is not of the perishable kind. So he dug the taxes out of a reluctant class pocket, and unrelentingly pursued the elusive ad to its lair. With too much sense for a fool, too matter-of-fact for genius, yet obviously miles from the average—"O for breath to utter what is like thee." His ponderosity we might expatiate upon—but why describe Vesuvius to the Neapolitans or pyramids to the Memphians?

name: Frederick Adam Hahn
motto: "The river glideth at his own sweet will." —Wordsworth.
description: This is Hahn, descendant of the fig-leaf man, alias game-cock; and he is a gamey little fellow, for even Joe Ames' Physics never for a moment made him lose his composure. He had his opinion of any man that would give such a course, but he confided it to only a select few. It was rich but unrepeatable. It is a pleasure to meet at times a man who does not give a damn what happens, and such a man is Hahn.

name: Hammond, Albert Lanphier
motto: "That lingerer."—Wordsworth.
clubs: Class Numerals, Varsity Tennis Team, So., J., S.
Winner in Doubles, Fall Tournament, S.
Chess Club, F., So., J., S.
Varsity Chess Team, F., So., J., S.
Secretary-Treasurer, Chess Club, So., President, J.
Grotius Society, F., So., J., S.
Adviser, Class and Varsity Debating Teams, S.
description: Firm, cool, complacent, not gay, perhaps, but cheerful. A profound reverence pervades all his conceptions of energy. Possessing a sober solemnity that at times borders on the ridiculous, he combines a Yankee reserve with a Southern dread of work. A rebellious administerer of honor systems, a launcher of enigmatical replies hopelessly beyond his questioners in class and out, he has the appearance of a grind, with the heart of a racket-wielder. Seeing both sides of a question with such clearness that at times he seemed to lack decision, he was the anchor to windward, the old familiar friend of 1914. But lo, this self-constituted champion of the weary, stands revealed a traitor to the cause. The hills from whence cometh our strength have been moved, and the mountains skipped like rams. The spirit of 1914 moved upon the face of the waters; Hammond heard and brought forth THE HULLABALOO. What if some, in turning the pages, smile and say "Parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus?"

name: Herman, Nathan Bernard
motto: "I am nothing if not critical." — Shakespeare.
description: Nathan Herman is like the busy bee who was tireless in the gathering of sweetness, though unlike the bee, he seems to detest what is really sweetest. He is a misogynist; to him women are dangers to be avoided. He refuses to be inveigled; he will devote his life to his work. On the experimental sciences he has bestowed his affections. Indeed it is sometimes said that he has but little toleration for that part of the world which happens to know less chemistry and biology than he—a vilipendency shown in an eternal clack of superior witticism.

name: Hilgartner, Andrew Henry
motto: "Hell is paved with good intentions."—Dr. Johnson.
clubs: Assistant Manager Baseball Team, So., J.
description: Every class must have its German comedian, and Hilgartner plays this part well, filling the vacancy left by Pieper. From the very first it was noticed that Andy made an interesting change in the curriculum of the University. Instead of studying a course, he would make a detail-study of the professor himself. Ah, but there was a method in his madness, for in this way the Class of 1914 developed probably the keenest "doper" of exams in the history of the University. He took an early interest in baseball, and now they let him manage the team. However he and Porter as captain undoubtedly would wreck the team, but for such fixed stars as Rice, Howell, Dorsey and others.

name: Guy Tilghman Orme Hollyday
motto: "Get money, still get money, boy; No matter by what means." —Ben Jonson. O cives, cives, quaerenda pecunia primum est. —Horace.
clubs: J., S.; Assistant Manager, F., So.; Vice-President, Y. M. C. A., S.; Varsity Lacrosse Team, J., S.; Class Lacrosse Team, F., So.; Class Football Team, F., So.; Class Vice-President, F.; Delegate, Athletic Board, J., S.; Delegate, Interfraternity Board, J., S.; Secretary, S.; Delegate, Cotillon Board, F., So., J.
description: And Hollyday has been getting money for the News-Letter since his arrival at college. He has invented many ingenious ways of making the fellows "come across;" and really he thinks himself so good that he is going to try to put his methods into practice in selling real estate next year. We make this announcement with some reluctance, knowing, to quote the Sun, that "all mothers of marriageable daughters (only of the best families in Baltimore) are deeply interested in his welfare," but feel that they should be fortified with the same knowledge that we possess. At odd moments he plays lacrosse with furious and irrepressible energy; but at odd moments only, for recently he has become greatly enamored with missionary work. Should he perchance enter this field, we honestly believe that he would prove O. K., for so far as we know (take heed marriageable daughters), he has never conducted himself improperly, but always walks the straight and narrow path. May he ever continue so to do.

name: Howell, Roger
motto: "Divine in hookas, glorious in a pipe, When tipped with amber, mellow, rich and ripe." —Byron.
clubs: Manager Varsity Lacrosse Team, J., S.
Member, Instrumental Club, So., J. S.
description: Here you see Roger of lacrosse-managerial fame. He has the reputation of having put through a season's schedule without a single naughty swear-word. However, we don't believe all we hear or all we put in print either. Under ordinary provocation 1914 does not like to raise a howell, but at the thought of Roger we just could not help it. He affects one of those elongated cigarette-holders in order to smoke under someone else's nose. Still we'll forgive him—he is so young. Besides being a star (the way to see a small star is not to look directly at it) in baseball, football, and lacrosse, Roger is also a crack at basketball. Just an all-round athlete, don't-cha-know! We have often wondered why he didn't run, yet his predilection for taking things easy might make track work seem too fast. As a musician Roger evokes everyone's sympathy, but he does his heavy work after the concerts. He is right there with the ladies! Maybe Roger means to be a doctor—poor public! EXTRA DRY 61

name: Samuel Jacob Keiser
motto: "Listen to my tale of woe. For three long years I worked so that I might get marks—marks that would make it possible to graduate with honor. So anxious was I to gain said honor that I helped considerably to increase Mr. Rockefeller’s income by burning the oil at midnight. But when I became a Senior, I suddenly realized that life outreaches the ken of the grind. It came about thus: I met Her one day while walking on the terrace. Ever since I have had no desire to study. I have become a smoker, a dreamer, and a lover of the theatre. To me it is now a pleasure to look upon the wine when it is red, to listen to song, and the face of a woman, especially a fair fair, fills my heart with awe. As to my future life, I shall continue to hold the Hebrew professorship which I now have. I may even try to acquire a similar position at Deichman’s or Hopkins, but in any case, teaching shall be but the means unto an end, and that end is Her."
description: I earn that I get, get that I wear, owe no man hate, envy no man’s happiness, glad of other men’s good, content with my harm.—Shakespeare.

name: Lauchheimer, Malcolm Horace
motto: "A little knowledge is a dangerous thing."—Pope.
clubs: Musical Clubs, F., So., J., S.
description: According to the Dean's Logic the obverse of the above would be: No little knowledge is a harmless thing. And here we have Malcolm Lauchheimer—he is of no little knowledge, and he is a harmless thing. How neatly and precisely is he summed up! It is but right that he should be so treated, for he himself is neat and precise. Both his appearance and his doings are measured and dainty. He is the pink of perfection.

name: Leibensperger, Raymond
motto: "Long is it as a barber's pole or mast of little boat."——Wordsworth.
clubs: Varsity Football Team, So., J., S.
Varsity Track Squad, F., So., S.
description: And now, patient reader, we approach the longest subject in this exhibit—yet we shall endeavor to be brief. Before we proceed to examine his giraffe-like Highness, let 1914 give him a "Long Ray with a daisy on the end." Ray's disposition is most amicable, and, in spite of his forbidding size, the smallest children, even Freshmen, may come near without danger. Ray was just one of us—then the coaches saw someone breaking things up in the Freshman-Sophomore game, and—well, now we see him chiefly from the grand-stand. But we've got your number Ray! Young ladies, listen: If there be one of you ambitious to have an humble servitor, just glance charmingly and smile sweetly thitherward; for this hulking six-foot-three, who can nonchalantly make husky classmates yell for mercy, will succumb immediately. He has been known to spot a female three blocks off, and cross to the other side of the street. Ray will probably be a geologist. Look out, old fellow, we've recently heard some skirts swishing about the laboratories.

name: Frank Kimball Leland
motto:
"Perhaps it may turn out a song,
Perhaps turn out a sermon."——Robert Burns.
clubs: Assistant Manager, the Musical Clubs, F.
Member, the Glee Club, F., So.
description: O good gray head which all men knew.—Tennyson. Old as I am, for ladies' love unfit, The power of beauty I remember yet.—Dryden. "How fortunate," says Addison, "is that company in which there is some older, wiser head to counsel the younger members." How doubly blessed then are we in the example and guidance of one who knows so well to "scorn delights and live laborious days," who so strictly avoids the corrupting touch of the world, who so firmly holds to all the intricacies of theological dogma. Behold our class model, our St. Anthony, our Thomas Aquinas, our Savonarola. But away with irony; let its acid edge seek fitter prey,—"Pop" is too cosmically jovial. What benevolence beams from his countenance and reverberates in that rich and soothing voice, with which he calms the troubled waters of student agitation and captivates the faculty. A delicious compound,—within the space of one hour he was seen to come tumbling gaily and triumphantly through the center of a large glass door, and was heard to deliver a most earnest and touching homily on cribbing in quizzes.

name: Levy, Charles Sumner
motto: "No beauty she doth miss When all her robes are on; But Beauty's self she is When all her robes are gone."——Anon.
description: Charles Sumner Levy is one of the most individual and versatile members of our class; Levy is Levy. On his shoulders he wears the mantle of wisdom, and from his lips come many witticisms. He, like many others in the Class of 1914, is a worshiper at the shrine of Aesculapius, and he will follow the path of Galen and Paracelsus (in plain United States, he is going to enter the Hopkins Medical School). Sumner, —oh Sumner, there is a romance in that name, but that is another story,—is little in stature, but great in voice. He has reported East Baltimore society functions, and spoken from a soapbox during the Presidential campaign. Yea, verily, he is a man of parts, and a prophet has arisen in our midst. He preaches the confusion of the mongers of patent medicines and fake health cures. And finally, he is one of the few, a devilish crew, that has gone through the J. H. U. in less time than yourself.

name: Lynch, C. Vernon
motto: "The pride of the Philistines."——Zechariah.
clubs: Recording Secretary, Y. M. C. A., So.
Vice-President, Executive Council, Y. M. C. A., J.
Member Glee Club, F., So., J., S.
description: We have on exhibition here, ladies and gentlemen, one of our most interesting specimens. Cast a glance on these sad and ascetic, yet at the same time upright and determined features, and draw your own conclusions as to the character of the man they belong to. Vernon is the most self-assured man in the class. Confident in his own superiority, he holds the even tenor of his ways and regards no man. The loud and positive way in which he used to volunteer information in the Dean's class won him great favor with the proprietor thereof, although in reality Vern was just as blissfully ignorant as the rest of us. In other classes the same thing held true, and when in years to come the Medical School will be favored by his presence, he will administer the wrong pills to the Dispensary patients with such domineering assurance that the poor victims will feel guilty if ever again in all their lives they experience another ache or pain. Vernon has taken courses in Biology hitherto followed by no undergraduate. However we can forgive both this and his singing in the Glee Club, in consideration of his general harmlessness.

name: McKenney, Samuel Mortimer
motto: "A tongue chained up without a sound." — J. Fletcher.
description: Mac doesn't drink, he doesn't swear, he doesn't smoke; think of a man receiving a degree for a liberal education and being so lacking in these things that go to make up a college-bred man! He comes from Glyndon, Baltimore's leading suburb, two hours and a half from the City Hall. Mac sincerely believes that he would make a good student if only he could keep awake during the lectures. Those large, soulful eyes always make one think that he is in love, when as a matter of fact he is a horrible flirt. He is one of those unfortunate individuals who are born with a kind heart and can't get rid of it. His other principal fault is that he minds his own business; but in spite of all this, he will probably succeed, owing to his ability to keep his mouth shut and look wise.

name: Nachlas, Israel William
motto: "Bid me discourse, I will enchant thine ear." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Member, Chess Club, F., So., J., S.
Member, Grotius Society, So., J., S.
Alternate, Grotius Society Intercollegiate Debating Team, J.
description: I, Nachlas, am a very unobtrusive little boy with light hair and blue eyes. Assiduous as I am in the pursuit of study, occasionally I take time to smile, to harmonize in some student chorus, and am always ready for a chess match catch-as-catch-can or blindfold. I was asked to describe myself because my bashfulness has made me seem a mystery to almost everyone. Yet on occasion I can wax both very eloquent and very bold, and am indeed an impassioned and inspired orator.

name: Powers, Donald Wayles
motto: "Tempt not the frailty of our Powers." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Winner Consolations, Fall Tennis Tournament, S.
description: In the Autumn of 1910, there wandered into town from Towson (this place is in Baltimore County, twelve miles out), Donald Wayles Powers. Donald had lived the life of a good boy in a quiet country rectory; and, in this his first walk alone in a great city, he was thrilled through and through by its sights and sounds. Reaching the Belvidere from Union Station, Donald was tempted to enter and indulge in a champagne cocktail. He'd gladly have gone, but his future Alma Mater called him onward to her more proper and sedate bosom. At Eager Street, on this momentous morning, Donald saw his first fire-engine, and stood in rapturous gaze. In Mount Vernon Place he met Allan Castleman, who told Donald not to be timid, for Allan had been in Baltimore before. Donald has been a good student, unobtrusively quiet, and the only indiscreet thing he has ever done while in the University was to have been seen once walking down Howard Street at midnight. Donald must have missed the last train for Towson.

name: Blanchard Randall, Jr.
motto: "In form and moving how express and admirable! A combination and a form indeed." —Shakespeare.
clubs: Class Track and Baseball Teams, So.
Glee Club, F., So., J., S.
description: A combination and a form indeed.

name: Rice, John Hubner
motto: "Claret is the liquor for boys, port for men, but he who aspires to be a hero must drink brandy."—— Samuel Johnson.
description: Some few years ago Hubner decided to leave the country and try his luck in the city. He had heard tell of the Johns Hopkins University and, after many doubts and misgivings, concluded that a degree from this college would be worth striving for. So one day, in the Fall, after many inquiries he came to McCoy Hall. Here he was greeted by a group of Sophomores, who doubtless saw the earmarks of the country upon this lad, although he had been diligently trying to get the hay out of his hair for some weeks. It required very little effort on the part of the Sophs to beguile Rice away, and really for a while he thought them very nice and courteous boys. Suffice it to say that he was initiated into the ways of the cruel world. It has been said that Hubner wakes up just once a year, along in the Spring when the baseball season begins. He has one great ambition in life—to play with the Federal League, and it is generally understood that several managers are looking him over. "He is a good hitter, but runs too much in the same place," according to Smoke Peiper.

name: Richmond, Harry W.
description: Harry is one of the most faithful frequenters of Levering Hall. Here he sits him down in the window-seats, unconscious that he is the "cynosure of neighboring eyes," and reads Life, The Cosmopolitan, or Woman's Bazaar. Frequently, however, he turns aside from these literary investigations, and debates with all comers on all subjects. Under the spell of his eloquence, the festive bun pauses midway to the gaping lips, as the listeners forget to eat, and wonder whether it would not be better to hurl their lunches,—buns, cake, and zweiback,—at the speaker's head. Harry ought to be an authority on Physics, having spent more than his allotted span being bulldozed by Uncle Joe. He is a proficient linguist, and converses with ease in Latin, Hebrew, Greek, etc., etc., but when talking with ordinary people he can be induced to speak English—and plenty of it.

name: Benjamin Rosen
motto: "Now Jove, in his next commodity of hair, send thee a beard." — Shakespeare.
description: Here we have the apostate and renegade member of the class. After having been permitted to taste the joys of becoming a member of our brilliant class, Bennie deliberately went and deserted to our sister college, Columbia. No words of scorn can be strong enough to express the feelings with which every true son of Hopkins ought to contemplate this satanic countenance. Nevertheless, the subject of this eulogy is in other respects quite human. For example, he had the fortitude, in his Junior year, to cut Joe Ames' lectures three times out of every two, and then to show that he didn't care a rap, he deliberately joined the army of Physics flunkers. Having gone in for Pedagogy, Bennie will soon have frequent opportunities to display his good sportsmanship in treating the feminine students who come under his protecting wing to cabaret shows and wine suppers. Enough for this traitor!

name: Sachs, Louis
motto: "Tantacne animis caelestibus irae?"—Virgil.
description: Behold, gentle reader (plagiarized from Thackeray), a young man endowed with the physical charms of an Adonis and the intellectual powers of a Socrates. His claims to carnal beauty rest upon the graceful manner in which he carries his five feet six inches, and upon the naive method of combing back his rich, black hair. In the vernacular, "he is a bear with the ladies." When he isn't talking about a party he attended or a dance he is going to attend, he drops gentle hints pertaining to his utter superiority over the rest of the world in matters scientific. As a logician, he has Aristotle and the Dean up a tree; but his habit of talking during the whole hour kept the Dean in ignorance of that fact. His literary hobby is Paris in the sixteenth century. His ambition, like that of the physician in Gil Blas, is to coin money by plying his prospective patients with hot poultices and frequent blood-lettings. One of our few mark-worshipers. Quite a character in his own opinion, but in no one else's. Next!

name: Schaffer, Aaron
motto: "He must live Knowing that he grows wiser day by day, Or else not live at all, and seeing, too, Each little drop of wisdom as it falls Into the dimpling cistern of his heart."——Wordsworth.
clubs: Member, Invitation Committee, Intercollegiate Peace Contest
description: Aaron is a very precocious puer who would like to appear wicked even though he isn't. Although he always knows the text-books verbatim, he insists that he never studies, but devotes his nights to wild, romantic, orgiastic pleasure. His eagerness in the study of languages and their literatures can, perhaps, be explained by the variety of naughty myths which are thus made available. He has a tenacious memory which treasures up these bits of classic cleverness. Aaron's attainments are indeed so considerable that it is said that he knows almost everything. But he does not cease utterly with knowledge. If there is a job to be done, leave it to Aaron. He will do it with a minimum of delay and fuss.

name: Scharf, Carl
motto: "The purposes of wisdom ask no more." —Wordsworth.
description: This little infant prodigy was raised on Munich beer and sauerkraut ever since he was a tiny shaver,—which is a strong argument for prohibition. In fact Carl is such a depraved chap that the only time that he gets to sleep is during the Psychology hour. Hank is so small that, when he takes a bath he exercises special precaution to get out of the tub before removing the stop-cock, for fear of getting a free passage to Jones' Falls. His encyclopedic knowledge enabled him to go through the University curriculum in three short years. He is, therefore, an intruder into the noble class of 1914, and deserves to be treated summarily. Get thee to a nunnery, Ophelia; hence, away, begone!

name: Schoenewolf, Arthur Martin
motto: "Rest is sweet."——Owen Meredith
description: Arthur M. Schoenewolf's general outline is most suggestive of a telegraph pole, but when he faces about you get the full benefit of his manly six-feet-two. His sylph-like, toothpick form does not interfere with his proficiency as a basketball shark, however, as the impotent rage of his opponents testifies when they watch him, leaning against the basket-supports, nonchalantly tipping in goals over their heads, with an air of infinite boredom. The main purpose of his Senior year seems to have been the demonstration of how to pass Biology II with a minimum of time spent in the laboratory. He might write a very helpful treatise upon that subject if he could spare the time from resting in Levering Hall. His general method is to stroll into the lab occasionally, hold a brief consultation with "Chen," sniff several times, and stroll back to Levering Hall. Good luck to you, Arts! May we be around when you wake up, as you will probably move some to make up for lost time.

name: Simpson, Kemper
motto: "Nursed on the food of opinion."——Plato."It would talk! Lord, how it would talk!"——Beaumont and Fletcher.
description: All hail! Kemper Simpson, our pride and delight, he is brilliant by day, but quite dazzling by night. When the banqueters sat round the well-ravaged board, then Simmy arose and forth there was poured such rhythmical knowledge of Joe and Ma Greene together with fashion-notes touching the Dean, we wondered, while loud rang the wild approbation, whether calm calculation, or recent potation, was the source of the poet's divine inspiration. For Kemper's a self-confessed hedonist,—well, yes, that means a fellow that is going to hell; 'tis a reagent dose for his knowledge-stocked mind, lest someone might slanderously call him a grind, with interest rapacious and memory tenacious he stores erudition in coffers capacious; his reading is truly prodigious, egregious,—to doubt Simmy's learning is quite sacrilegious. He delights in authority, revels in encyclopedias and worships the gods of the pen. He heard of the Adams Prize—books,—and at once he got up and demolished the "god of the Munsey" to such good effect that the judges spoke thusly: "The debate is the Juniors', unanimously." An orthodox paradox-retailing knave, here's to his chubbyness, long may he wave!

name: Snyder, Charles Irwin
motto: "I love the shower's descending beat."—Longfellow.
description: What shall we say of Charles Irwin Snyder? It is a pleasure to write of this man, for you know him—he is easily approached, of a sociable nature, and a good friend. To hear Snyder talk, you'd think him hell on wheels when he goes out to celebrate after exams are over, but he lacks that twinkle in his eye which is a dead give-away to a real sport; and, moreover, no one has ever seen Snyder out after dark excepting at a class banquet. His two chief topics of conversation during his four years at college have been "Mother" Green, and where "Pop" Leland, "Dewey" Penniman, "Frankie" La Motte, "Andy" Hilgartner, and some more of that bunch, spend their nights. Snyder may be a sport yet, for he has a restless spirit and is a boon companion of Cochran Supplee, a dangerous combination. He and Cochran study together, go to the theatre together, take baths together, sleep together—no, I didn't say drink together, for neither one of them knows what a cocktail looks like, though it is thought that they smoke together in the secrecy of their rooms.

name: Steinmueller, Karl Josef
motto: "Now, by two-headed Janus, Nature hath framed strange fellows in her time." —Shakespeare.
description: Karl Josef Steinmueller has grown wonderfully since his association with his Alma Mater. From one of the shortest members of the class, he has grown in the last three years to be one of the tallest; from a modest, retiring youth has been evolved a bold, bad man who can tango, etc., with the best. He is a linguist, and he also has the gift of gab. His persuasive, honey words induce people to buy dictating machines, typewriter supplies, magazines, and News-Letter and HULLABALOO advertising. Proclaim it from the housetops, let the whole city keep quiet and tremble; for in the space of four years will be loosed upon the citizens of Baltimore Dr. Karl Josef Steinmueller. And incidentally he will conduct researches on the vocal cords of the denizens of the backfences. The crystal sphere now becomes clouded, and the future is uncertain. Karl Josef, as old Sulphuric loves to call him, has inherited his progenitors' musical talent, and he tenderly plays the bass viol, caressing therefrom dulcet notes. Hoch soll er leben.

name: Stiebel, Harold Leo
motto: "He hath never fed of the dainties that are bred in a book; he hath not eat paper, as it were; he hath not drunk ink."-Shakespeare.
clubs: Varsity Track Team, So., J., S., Track "H.," So., J., S.
description: "Smoke" Stiebel is some runner, and has figured on all sorts of relay teams. One reason for his success is that he wears an expression of such agony, as though tortured by extreme pain (or "unpleasantness" as Dr. Dunlap calls it) in his digestive regions, that his opponents pause to look for medical assistance for him. Meanwhile, Harold, tortoise-like, plods on and gains a big lead. In the class-room his answers are extremely original, and are prefaced by the invariable formula, "I'm not sure I know, but—." The duet, "The Marseillaise," which he sang with "Pop" Leland at the Sophomore banquet lingered long in the minds of all unfortunate enough to hear it. After carefully considering all his characteristics, including the size of his feet, we suggest that he would make an admirable addition to the police force.

name: Cochran Supplee
motto: "The expectancy and rose of the fair state."——Shakespeare.
description: Cochran Supplee is noted for his wit and for his personal charms. Speaking of his wit, some contend that one half of it must have been stolen before we knew him. Be that as it may, he seems to enjoy exercising the half that remains. His physical charms are inherited; he comes of a family of masculine pulchritude. The greatest element in both of these attributes is his smile; for when he smiles we realize that he thinks he has said something funny, and it is then, too, that the beauty of his person is radiant with added glory.

name: Sutton, Walter Degges
motto: "Hi there, Terry! Have a bud? Hello Smoke! Looking pale around the gills? Swallow one of these!"
description: Comb down his hair. Look, look, it stands on end!—Shakespeare. It is a safe bet that thirty seconds later chocolate buds will be flying through the air. We can't think of Butts without thinking of buds. Buddy Butts! Butts has played lacrosse; in fact we made him captain of the team this year; and we have seen him in a Varsity baseball suit; but his greatest fame rests upon his performances as a mile-runner, football player, and public speaker. We sincerely hope he has no German or French ancestors, for they would certainly be restless under ground. When Congress makes it a crime to murder a foreign tongue, Butts had better move to Canada. He is forever in a good humor exasperating enough to more irritable mortals. Altogether he is a worthless kind of chap. This is our honest opinion, but we felt it would be unkind to let him know it during the college course. Therefore, as you can see above, we made him President of the Senior Class.

name: Del Valle, Carlos M.
motto: "What miracle of weird transforming Is this wild work?—Whittier."
description: He has absorbed with great avidity all that Mercersburg Academy and J. H. U. can impart to an aspiring mind. The Medical School is looking forward to having him within its walls next year. His later career is still undecided. He does not know whether to become a professional bull-fighter, or to start a revolution in his native land in imitation of General Huerta. In either case his speed of foot, and his medical knowledge are likely to prove useful. He really is a very progressive personality, his favorite exercises being running and walking. The former he performs in the cage; the latter on Lexington Street.

name: Weber, Carl Jefferson
motto: "Ah! what would the world be to us If the children were no more?"—Longfellow.
clubs: Associate Editor, The News-Letter, J., S.
Honorable Mention, F., So., J., S.
Member, Y. M. C. A., F., So., J., S.
Member, Bible Class Committee, F., So., J.
Member, Instrumental Club, F., So., J., S.
Member, Glee Club, So., J., S.
Member, Grotius Society, So., J., S.
Adviser, Grotius Society Intercollegiate Debating Team, J.
Head Usher, Grotius Zelosophic Intercollegiate Debate, J.
Adviser, Junior Team in Interclass Debate, J.
1914 Maryland Rhodes Scholar to Oxford, England, S.
description: Carl J Weber

name: Clifford Almon Wells
motto: "Sentimentally disposed to harmony, but organically incapable of a tune." —Lamb.
clubs: Captain Class Track Team, F., So.
description: This is the Briton primeval, Canadian subject of King George, Come from the North to learn the lore of the civilized Southland. Loyal he is to the infant Dominion and England the senile, Yea, though full four years he has basked in our glorious sunlight,— Why do the heathen rage and the people imagine a vain thing? Cliff himself would have put this in stateliest Latin dactylics, But he alone of us all is a rival of Virgil and Horace. French, too, he loves to speak, with an accent delightfully British,— So say the papers of Paris in telling the world of his visit. There in the purlieus of Paris he picked up the taste for tobacco, Even, speak softly and low, is said to have yielded to un bock. Dark-haired he is, and strong, like a man of times prehistoric; Runner who ran the mile as training for running the quarter; Punner of villainous puns, far-fetched and abysmally British. Maidens may wear their piquant hobble or sauciest slit-skirt, Cliff with a fearful wisdom avoids all feminine ambush. This is the Briton primeval, our only Canadian exhibit.

name: Steuart D'ohl White
motto: "mens conscia recti"
clubs: Member, Glee Club, F., So., J., S.
Member Varsity Quartet, F., So., J., S.
Π K X, Φ B K
description: Our matador of virtue, sans peur et sans reproche; exemplary rectitude, probity inviolate and inviolable, rampant and irrepressible conscientiousness gleam from his eye and radiate from his stalwart form. The Boy Napoleon, the Little Giant goes forth indomitably to beard the contumacious Lords of Evil,—and the greatest of these is idleness. At work or play he spares not a moment, is ever industrious, in harness, intent, eager, enterprising, zealous, strenuous, in earnest, diligent, sedulous, painstaking, business-like, assiduous, indefatigable. The variety of his activity equals its vigor; he is preacher, debater, and orator in general, scholar, athlete, and musician,—a tenor that follows the tenor tradition only in being unlike all other tenors. His motto is "mens conscia recti," and his watchword "nec mora nec requies."

name: Whitehill, Milford Henry
motto: "Yond Cassius hath a lean and hungry look; Would he were fatter." —Shakespeare.
description: Milford Whitehill seems to exist merely to show the world that there is something as thin as Professor Rowland's famous spectrum gratings. Indeed he is so thin that a description of him must be given, for there is a possibility that his photograph has slipped away from these pages. He is neat and sleek; he is well-groomed and well-oiled. His tight-fitting English jackets reduce him from pencil dimensions to the thickness of a line. It has been said that he is much sought after by the young girls. The subtle charm of his manner is, perhaps, accountable.

name: Lawson Wilkins
motto: "His tongue Dropt manna, and could make the worse appear The better reason." —Milton.
clubs: Delegate, Inter-Fraternity Board, So., J., S.
description: We doubt not that every class has its wily, winsome, winning wheedler. Here we exhibit our never-known to fail campaigner,—his mellifluous, unctuous tone would be sufficient to soothe Joe Ames in one of his worst distempers. Getz is his only rival, with this difference: Lawrence makes you mad, while Lawson—well, he makes you sick. But honest now, this chap could persuade you that an elephant has two tails and then prove that you were lying. We wish Lawson might have lived more among us. At one time he started to play lacrosse, and we thought we had discovered an embryonic star. But he gave it up and seemed better pleased to shoot 10's than lacrosse goals. His aim has been accurate. This ingenious brother's chief amusement is gained by exhibiting nauseating biological specimens to those who may be working over "Life" at the windows of Levering Hall. He has also an appreciative eye for the high school lassies who daily trip across our campus.

name: Yost, John Stevenson Long
motto: "Though I am not splenetive and rash, Yet have I something in me dangerous."——Shakespeare.
description: He is the fellow you noticed on your first day at the University; he is the fellow you have noticed every day of your four years of college life, and he is the last fellow you notice when you leave. Johnny is an interesting character—no one has ever fathomed him. His smile is pleasing—his scowl is terrible. When he speaks you may not pay much attention to him until you are startled by some unexpected words of wisdom, and then you look upon Johnny as Socrates reincarnated. Johnny looks something like an ancient Greek philosopher, anyway. He has been a great friend of Alex Barton, and, between them, some of the direst of plots and the weightiest of questions have been laid and solved. Johnny would make a good school teacher, for he has a schoolmaster's authoritative voice and terrifying manner—yet Johnny really has a sweet and gentle nature. Lower classmen have always stood in awe of him, however, as he looks to them like a veteran of Bunker Hill with "Don't tread on me" emblazoned upon his face.
